content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural grade reorganization (lower grades stay, upper grades centralize)'. Hungary's statutory framework grants maintainers broad discretion to close or reorganize rural primary schools, but multiple procedural safeguards (annual implementation deadlines, mandatory consultation, phase-out options, maintainer-change alternatives, and political-legal caution) collectively slow the speed and frequency of closures below what legal authority alone would permit.
opportunities: Enables full-time specialist subject teaching at the centralized upper-grade site, addressing the documented Polish problem of rural teachers commuting between multiple part-time postings.

Preserves a local school presence for the youngest, most travel-vulnerable children (grades 1-4/1-6), partially mitigating the documented burden of early-morning long-distance travel for young pupils.
strengths: The National Public Education Act (Nkt. 2011. évi CXC. törvény) establishes that primary schools can be maintained by the state, municipalities, nationality self-governments, church legal entities, or authorized private organizations, creating a formal plurality of maintainers (fenntartók) with distinct legal identities and decision authority.

School reorganization or closure decisions by state-maintained institutions (tankerület) must be decided no later than the last working day of May for implementation in the following school year, creating a hard annual procedural deadline that structurally limits the speed of closure implementation.
threats: Poland's 1999 reform was politically reversible and was in fact reversed after 17 years; without concrete cross-party entrenchment mechanisms or constitutional-level protection, Hungary's version faces similar reversal risk, especially given the documented absence of any cross-party consensus mechanism for rural school reform.

Teacher redeployment under Act LII of 2023 (Status Law), which grants district centers authority to reassign teachers, may trigger the kind of mobilization seen in the Tanítanék movement if perceived as involuntary displacement without adequate consultation.
weaknesses: No primary legislative source or government regulatory document was located that establishes an explicit statutory minimum enrollment threshold (e.g., 'a school with fewer than N students must close' or 'must be reorganized'), as exists in some comparative jurisdictions; it is unclear whether such thresholds operate as informal administrative practice or guidance without statutory force.

The scope and current enforceability of the pre-2010 requirement for independent expert assessment before school closure is not confirmed in force or codified in the current Nkt. or subordinate regulations; it is unclear whether this procedural gate remains mandatory, optional, or obsolete.
